role_summary: Saint Augustine, Florida native; witness to a Ku Klux Klan parade through the Lincolnville Black neighborhood as a child; one of the African American students who integrated previously all-white St. Augustine public schools in the late 1960s; later educator and non-profit manager; participant in the St. Augustine ACCORD Freedom Trail commemoration project.
ai_reading: Duncan's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 94), with Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (entry 103) as the top match and the broader cluster including Barbara Edna Vickers (entry 5), Purcell Maurice Conway (entry 99), and the joint Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer interview (entry 4). The Hayling pairing is the catalog's clearest St. Augustine civil-rights-movement cosine: Hayling led the NAACP Youth Council and brought King to St. Augustine in 1964; Vickers organized alongside Hayling as his neighbor; Duncan was a child during that organizing arc and integrated the schools that resulted from it. The catalog flags a +0.89 reading toward Armed Self-Defense on the nonviolence axis; for a 1956-born St. Augustine resident whose civil rights memory is shaped by witnessing KKK violence in childhood, the embedding's pull toward Self-Defense is consistent with a witnessing-to-violence register that places her testimony in continuity with the catalog's documented St. Augustine Hayling-Vickers cohort.
biographical_paragraph: While Duncan's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 51 [src: 1, src: 2]. Duncan was born in 1956 in Saint Augustine, Florida and grew up in the city's historically Black Lincolnville neighborhood. As a child she witnessed a Ku Klux Klan parade through Lincolnville, the kind of segregationist intimidation event the early-1960s St. Augustine civil rights organizing (led by Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling, catalog entry 103, alongside Barbara Edna Vickers, catalog entry 5) was responding to [src: 2]. She was one of the African American students who integrated previously all-white St. Augustine schools in the late 1960s. She attended St. Johns River College, married Richard Allen Duncan in 1976, and has five children. She worked across her career as an educator and in non-profit management. She participated in the ACCORD Freedom Trail initiative, the St. Augustine civil-rights-movement-commemoration project that documents and marks the sites of the local 1963-1964 organizing campaign [src: 2].
